Overview Quick Start Interactive Samples Root Schema example extensions link metadata presentation purpose rich-text status component pattern style theme token accessibility anatomy api best-practice content design-specifications guideline interaction motion principle scale state variant
Quick Start Guide

Everything you need to start writing machine-readable design system documentation in 5 minutes.

What is DSDS?

DSDS (Design System Documentation Standard) is a JSON-based format for documenting design systems. It gives every piece of documentation — components, tokens, themes, styles, patterns — a predictable, machine-readable structure.

Key idea: DSDS documents the how and why of your design system — not the token values themselves. It complements the W3C Design Tokens Format which handles the what. What you get
  • Structured — every section has a defined shape, no guessing
  • Machine-readable — tools can parse, generate, validate, and transform it
  • Portable — not locked to any documentation tool or platform
  • Extensible — add vendor metadata without breaking interoperability
  • Validatable — JSON Schema catches errors before they reach consumers
Document Structure

A DSDS file is a JSON document with a version and one or more documentation groups. Each group contains an array of typed entities.

{ "dsdsVersion": "0.1", "purpose": { "description": "Provides shared UI foundations for all Acme products.", "useCases": [ { "description": "Building customer-facing Acme web apps.", "kind": "positive" }, { "description": "Internal tooling with its own visual language.", "kind": "negative" } ] }, "bestPractices": [ { "guidance": "Always use semantic tokens instead of raw values.", "rationale": "Semantic tokens enable theming and reduce maintenance cost.", "kind": "required" } ], "documentation": [ { "name": "My Design System", "items": [ { "kind": "component", "name": "button", "...": "" }, { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-primary", "...": "" }, { "kind": "theme", "name": "dark", "...": "" } ] } ] }

That's the entire top-level structure:

  • dsdsVersion — always "0.1" for this version
  • purpose — optional object describing what the design system is for, with positive and negative useCases
  • bestPractices — optional array of cross-cutting rules that apply system-wide (e.g., "always use semantic tokens")
  • documentation — array of named groups, each with an items array
  • items — array of typed entities, mixed freely in any order
  • extends — optional object declaring that this document extends another DSDS document (typically a core design system). Individual entities can also declare their own extends to trace back to a base component or token. See the full spec for details.
Tip: You can organize entities into multiple groups — one for foundations, one for components, etc. — or put everything in a single group. The schema allows both.
Entity Types

Every entity has a kind discriminator that tells tools what type of thing it is. There are six kinds.

component "component"

A reusable UI element — buttons, inputs, modals. Supports anatomy, API, variants, states, and design specs.

token "token"

A single design token — color, spacing, typography. Carries a resolved value, platform API mappings, and usage rules.

token-group "token-group"

A hierarchical group of related tokens. Recursive — groups can contain groups.

theme "theme"

A named set of token value overrides — dark mode, high-contrast, compact density, brand variants.

style "style"

A macro-level visual foundation — color, typography, spacing, elevation. Carries principles, scales, and motion definitions.

pattern "pattern"

A broad interaction pattern — navigation, error messaging, empty states. Describes how components compose to solve a user need.

Common properties

Every entity shares these fields:

Property Required Description
kind Yes Entity type discriminator
name Yes* Machine-readable identifier (^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$, except tokens)
displayName Yes* Human-readable label
description Yes* What this entity is and does (CommonMark supported)
status No "stable", "experimental", "draft", "deprecated" — or an object with per-platform status
guidelines No Array of typed guideline objects (see below)
links No External resources and entity relationships
$extensions No Vendor-specific metadata

* Required for component, style, and pattern. Token requires name + tokenType + value.

Status: string or object

For the simple case, status is just a string:

{ "status": "stable" }

When you need per-platform tracking:

{ "status": { "overall": "stable", "platforms": { "react": { "status": "stable", "since": "1.0.0" }, "ios": { "status": "experimental", "since": "3.0.0" } } } }
The Guidelines System

All structured documentation lives in the guidelines array on each entity. Each guideline is a typed container identified by kind.

kind What it documents Available on
"purpose" When to use / when not to use All
"best-practices" Actionable rules with rationale All
"accessibility" Keyboard, ARIA, contrast, motion All
"content" Labels, terminology, localization All
"anatomy" Named parts with token references Component, Pattern
"api" Props, events, slots, CSS hooks Component
"variants" Enum + flag dimensions of variation Component, Pattern
"states" Interactive states with token overrides Component, Pattern
"design-specifications" Spacing, sizing, typography, responsive Component
"principles" High-level guiding beliefs Style
"scale" Ordered token progressions Style
"motion" Easing curves and durations Style
"interactions" Pattern flow steps Pattern
Purpose: when to use it

The purpose guideline provides concrete scenarios. Each use case is "positive" (do use) or "negative" (don't use, with an alternative):

{ "kind": "purpose", "useCases": [ { "description": "When the user needs to trigger an action such as submitting a form.", "kind": "positive" }, { "description": "When the action navigates to a different page.", "kind": "negative", "alternative": { "name": "link", "rationale": "Links carry native navigation semantics." } } ] } Best practices: how to use it

Each best practice pairs a guidance statement with a rationale and an enforcement level:

{ "kind": "best-practices", "items": [ { "guidance": "Limit each surface to one primary button.", "rationale": "Multiple primary buttons dilute visual hierarchy.", "kind": "required", "category": "visual-design" } ] }

Enforcement levels: required (MUST), encouraged (SHOULD), informational (MAY, default), discouraged (SHOULD NOT), prohibited (MUST NOT).

Minimal Examples

These are the smallest valid entities — the floor of documentation. Copy one, fill in your content, and add guidelines as you go.

Component

A component needs kind, name, displayName, and description. Everything else is optional.

{ "kind": "component", "name": "button", "displayName": "Button", "description": "A clickable element that triggers an action.", "status": "stable", "category": "action", "guidelines": [ { "kind": "purpose", "useCases": [ { "description": "When the user needs to trigger an action such as submitting a form or confirming a dialog.", "kind": "positive" }, { "description": "When the action is navigation to another page.", "kind": "negative", "alternative": { "name": "link", "rationale": "Links communicate navigation; buttons communicate actions." } } ] } ] } Token

A token needs kind, name, tokenType, and a value with at least resolved.

{ "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-primary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#1a1a1a" } } Token Group

Groups organize tokens into hierarchies. The children array can contain tokens or nested groups.

{ "kind": "token-group", "name": "color-text", "description": "Semantic text color tokens.", "status": "stable", "tokenType": "color", "category": "semantic", "children": [ { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-primary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#1a1a1a" }, "api": { "cssCustomProperty": "--color-text-primary" } }, { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-secondary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#6b7280" }, "api": { "cssCustomProperty": "--color-text-secondary" } }, { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-disabled", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#9ca3af" }, "api": { "cssCustomProperty": "--color-text-disabled" } } ] } Theme

A theme provides overrides — an array of token + value pairs that replace the default.

{ "kind": "theme", "name": "dark", "displayName": "Dark Mode", "description": "Dark color mode for reduced-light environments.", "status": "stable", "category": "color-mode", "overrides": [ { "token": "color-background-default", "value": { "resolved": "#1a1a1a" } }, { "token": "color-text-primary", "value": { "resolved": "#f5f5f5" } }, { "token": "color-border-default", "value": { "resolved": "#333333" } } ] } Style

A style documents a visual foundation. Guidelines like principles, scale, and motion are specific to styles.

{ "kind": "style", "name": "spacing", "displayName": "Spacing", "description": "The spatial system governing layout rhythm and component density.", "status": "stable", "guidelines": [ { "kind": "principles", "items": [ { "title": "Use the scale", "description": "Always pick a value from the spacing scale. Avoid arbitrary pixel values." } ] } ] } Pattern

A pattern describes how components compose to solve a user need. It supports interactions for flow steps.

{ "kind": "pattern", "name": "form-validation", "displayName": "Form Validation", "description": "Validates user input on form submission and communicates errors inline.", "status": "stable", "links": [ { "kind": "component", "name": "form-field", "role": "Displays inline error messages", "required": true }, { "kind": "component", "name": "button", "role": "Triggers form submission", "required": true }, { "kind": "component", "name": "toast", "role": "Confirms successful submission" } ] } Full document

Here's a complete, minimal DSDS file with one of each entity type:

{ "$schema": "../schema/dsds.bundled.schema.json", "dsdsVersion": "0.1", "metadata": { "systemName": "Minimal Example" }, "documentation": [ { "name": "Minimal Design System", "items": [ { "kind": "component", "name": "button", "displayName": "Button", "description": "A clickable button for triggering actions.", "status": "stable" }, { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-primary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#1a1a1a" } }, { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-secondary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#6b7280" } }, { "kind": "token-group", "name": "color-text", "children": [ { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-primary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#1a1a1a" } }, { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-secondary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#6b7280" } } ] }, { "kind": "theme", "name": "dark", "displayName": "Dark Mode", "description": "Dark color mode for low-light environments.", "status": "stable", "overrides": [ { "token": "color-text-primary", "value": { "resolved": "#f5f5f5" } }, { "token": "color-text-secondary", "value": { "resolved": "#a1a1aa" } } ] }, { "kind": "style", "name": "color", "displayName": "Color", "description": "The color system governing palette usage, semantic mapping, and accessibility.", "status": "stable" }, { "kind": "pattern", "name": "form-validation", "displayName": "Form Validation", "description": "Validates user input and communicates errors on form submission.", "status": "stable" } ] } ] }
Validate Your Document

DSDS includes a JSON Schema you can use to validate any document.

Using the bundled schema

Point your $schema at the bundled schema for editor autocompletion and inline validation:

{ "$schema": "https://designsystemdocspec.org/v0.1/dsds.bundled.schema.json", "dsdsVersion": "0.1", "documentation": [] } Using the CLI # Clone the repo git clone https://github.com/somerandomdude/design-system-documentation-schema.git cd design-system-documentation-schema npm install # Validate the built-in examples npm run validate # Validate your own file npx ajv validate -s spec/schema/dsds.bundled.schema.json -d my-system.dsds.json
Next Steps

You've seen the basics. Here's where to go deeper.

Resource What you'll find
Specification Site Full schema reference with property tables, types, and cross-references
Interactive Samples Side-by-side JSON ↔ rendered documentation for every entity type
Schema Architecture Visual diagram of how all schema files relate
Full Specification The complete spec — document structure, all entity types, all guidelines, naming conventions
All Examples Validated example files for every entity and guideline type
Getting started recipe:
  1. Copy the minimal document above
  2. Replace the example entities with your own design system's components and tokens
  3. Add guidelines to each entity as your documentation matures
  4. Validate with npm run validate to catch structural errors early

Design System Documentation Standard (DSDS) 0.1 — Quick Start Guide
